coredata.dc:title: Green and high-yield synthesis of carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent determination of pH and enzyme reactions
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yinyin
Zhao, Chenxi
Wang, Yanying
Rao, Hanbing
Lu, Zhiwei
Lu, Changfang
Shan, Zhi
Ren, Bi
Wu, Wei
Wang, Xianxiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with chitosan as a carbon source and hydrogen peroxide as an oxidant, this route is a novel, simple and green. The prepared CDs has a relatively high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) (18.9%) and good water solubility. CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) possess the characteristic of responding to pH rapidly. So a ratiometric fluorescent probe for measuring pH was constructed using the prepared CDs and CdTe QDs. Under the excitation of 330 nm, the probe shows dual (blue and red) emission with peaks at 430 and 600 nm, respectively. When the pH of the solution changes, the blue fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains stable as a reference signal, while the red fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs changes accordingly. This method has obvious pH-sensitive feature, stable fluorescence properties, and a great linear relationship with pH values in the range of pH 3.0–11.0. What is more, the method can monitor proton-producing enzyme-catalyzed reactions by acting as a ratiometric pH probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
CdTe quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Determination
pH
Enzyme reactions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CdTe QDs
Fluorescent assay of pH in buffer solutions
Characterization of CDs and CdTe QDs
Feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescence system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM of the CDs (a) and CdTe QDs (c) (Inset: crystal lattice). The particle size distribution histograms of CDs (b) and CdTe QDs (d).
The full range XPS scanning spectrum (a) and the high XPS spectra resolution of O 1s (b), N 1s (c), and C 1s (d) of the CDs.

Absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs (a) and fluorescence spectra CDs excited from 270 to 430 nm (b). Absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the CdTe QDs (c), Absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed system of CDs and QDs (d). Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in PBS buffer at different pH values (e) and pH-dependence of fluorescence emission spectra for CdTe QDs (f). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The fluorescence responses of the CDs/CdTe QDs to different interferences. (Calf thymus DNA 1 μg/mL, BSA, amides and amino acid 10 μM) (a). The fluorescence spectra of CDs/CdTe QDs in PBS buffer at different pH values (on the left of (b) Inset: fluorescent images of ratiometric fluorescent probe in pH range from 3.0 to 11.0.) and the linear relationship for ratio of fluorescence intensity vs. pH value from 3.0 to 11.0 (on the right of (b)).

Fluorescence spectra of the CDs/CdTe QDs for monitoring glucose oxidation catalyzed by GOx (1 mg/mL) from 0 to 60 mM at a constant temperature of 37 °C (inset: fluorescence photos in the monitoring of enzymatic reactions) (a). Relationship between I600/I430 and glucose concentration (black spot) and fitting data (b). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Hydrothermal preparation of CDs and construction of pH ratiometric fluorescence probes with CdTe QDs for monitoring enzyme catalytic reactions.

head.ce:title: Green and high-yield synthesis of carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent determination of pH and enzyme reactions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Agronomy, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Agronomy, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Crop Gene Exploration and Utilization in Southwest China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with chitosan as a carbon source and hydrogen peroxide as an oxidant, this route is a novel, simple and green. The prepared CDs has a relatively high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) (18.9%) and good water solubility. CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) possess the characteristic of responding to pH rapidly. So a ratiometric fluorescent probe for measuring pH was constructed using the prepared CDs and CdTe QDs. Under the excitation of 330 nm, the probe shows dual (blue and red) emission with peaks at 430 and 600 nm, respectively. When the pH of the solution changes, the blue fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains stable as a reference signal, while the red fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs changes accordingly. This method has obvious pH-sensitive feature, stable fluorescence properties, and a great linear relationship with pH values in the range of pH 3.0–11.0. What is more, the method can monitor proton-producing enzyme-catalyzed reactions by acting as a ratiometric pH probe.

Carbon dots were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with chitosan as a carbon source and hydrogen peroxide as an oxidant, and a ratiometric fluorescence was constructed for determination of pH and enzyme reactions based on carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Solution pH is a key parameter in many important physiological processes such as cell metabolism, proliferation, ion transport, signal transduction and apoptosis [1,2]. Minor changes in intracellular pH can affect the progression or completion of chemical reactions of cells and organelles, leading to organ damage or neurological problems [3]. Therefore, it is great significance to measure pH accurately. Routine pH detection methods include pH test strips [4,5] and electrochemical methods such as voltammetric [6,7], amperometric [8,9] and potentiometric methods [10]. There are some limitations in the use of pH electrodes, including the need for reference electrodes and frequent calibration, as well as the signal being susceptible to interference and instability. The pH test strips are used frequently, but the measured value is low precision. Therefore, these methods are not suitable for the intracellular pH value admeasurement. In this case, a lot of new methods for pH detection were developed. Optical methods have drawn increasing attention as a result of their higher sensitivity, selectivity, and simple operation. In addition, the optical probe method is a non-contact method that is more suitable for pH monitoring at the cellular level [11]. CDs, as a novel type of fluorescent nanomaterials, also shine in the application of pH sensing systems due to their excellent biocompatibility, light stability and ease of preparation [12–15]. Recently, Zhou et al. [16] synthesized CDs with concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid to detect heavy metal ion, and Zhang et al. [17] prepared double-emitting carbon dots with sodium hydroxide for pH sensing. However, the synthesis conditions of these CDs are relatively harsh and danger, because strong corrosive chemicals such as strong acids and strong bases were used, which easily cause serious environmental problems and cause hidden safety hazards.

Among the reported fluorescent methods, ratiometric fluorescence is particularly noticeable due to its outstanding characteristics [18–21]. Some researchers have even used ratiometric fluorescent paper strip to develop novel detection methods using smartphones [22–24]. Compared with the traditional single fluorescent channel, it can eliminate the error of the ratio of two fluorescent peaks and avoid the interference of some factors, such as the fluctuation of the excitation source and the concentration of the probe part, so as to obtain more reliable data and improve detection sensitivity [25–27]. There are some ratiometric fluorescent methods were reported. For example, Xiong produced near-infrared ratiometric fluorescent probes based on spiropyran derivatives, with the prominent features of photostability [22], large Stokes shift, and high selectivity, were used to evaluate cellular pH and cell imaging [28]. Yuan et al. developed two-photon (TP) ratiometric fluorescent probes for tracking lysosomal pH changes in living cells and zebrafish [29]. Besides, Wang et al. had reported the use of multiple CDs to detect pH value and monitor enzyme catalytic reactions [30]. However, the pH detection range is narrow and limited to 3.3–7.5. So, it is very necessary to develop a more moderate and simple method for preparing CDs and expand the response range of pH to monitor the proton-producing enzymatic reaction.

In this work, we proposed an innovative and facile way to synthesize CDs, and the fluorescence emission peak of carbon dot is at 430 nm. Compared with the traditional CDs synthesis method, the CDs synthesized by the mild oxidation of diluted H2O2 solution have higher fluorescence quantum yield. (Generally, the quantum yield of CDs synthesized by traditional hydrothermal methods is less than 10%). And H2O2 is an eco-friendly and safer reagent than other strong acid and alkali. Meanwhile, CdTe QDs with fluorescence emission at 600 nm were synthesized as a ratio probe. The fluorescence of CDs does not respond to pH changes, while the fluorescence of CdTe QDs obviously decreases with pH value decreasing. Based on this, a ratiometric fluorescence method for pH was constructed. This method also was applied to monitor proton-producing enzyme reactions successfully. The experimental process is shown in Scheme 1 .

Chitosan (CTS) was purchased from Jinan Haidebei Marine Bioengineering Co., Ltd. Tellurium grain and reduced L-glutathione were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Cadmium chloride, hydrogen peroxide, sodium borohydride and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Ke-long Chemicals Co., Ltd. PBS buffer was obtained from Solarbio Science & technology Co., Ltd. All the above reagents are analytical grade and can be used in the experiments without further purification.

All the pH measurements were performed using a model pHS-3E pH meter which obtained from INESA Science Instrument Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fluorescence emission spectrum were obtained from a F-4500 Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on an Aoyi A-390 spectrometer with 1.0 cm path length. The morphology and sizes of CDs were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL 2100F, Japan). ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer FT-IR Spectrum Two, China) were used to analyze and identify elements and functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared from chitosan as carbon source by a one−pot hydrothermal method. First, 1.0 g chitosan was added into a mixture of 16 mL of water and 4 mL of hydrogen peroxide and the mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After that, the autoclave was held in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h. Then, the autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature. The sediment was removed and the supernatant was filtered with a microporous membrane filter (0.22 μm). The obtained CDs were stored in refrigerator for further use.

CdTe QDs was synthesized according to a literature method with modified slightly [31]. 100 mL CdCl2·2H2O (2.5 mM) was added into 250 mL three-neck bottle, followed by adding 0.07683 g of GSH as a stabilizer and stirring constantly. After stirring for a period, the pH was adjusted to 8 with NaOH. Nitrogen gas was introduced into the system to remove dissolved oxygen. Then prepared freshly NaHTe with tellurium powder and sodium borohydride was quickly injected into the solution. The solution was refluxed for 90 min and cooled to room temperature. Finally, unreacted impurities were removed by centrifugation to obtain GSH-coated CdTe QDs. More importantly, GSH and Cd2 + have good chelation and can be used to resolve Cd2 + poisoning, so CdTe QDs have more extensive applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent assay of pH in buffer solutions
ce:section.ce:para: A range of standard pH buffers were prepared with 0.01 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) regulated with NaOH or HCl (2.0 M). Next, 1 mL PBS at various pH values and 1 mL of CDs and QDs solution were mixed separately. The volume ratio of red-emitting CdTe QDs and blue-emitting CDs is 4:1 (Fig. S5). Then, transferred the resulting solution to a quartz cuvette with an optical length of 1 cm. The fluorescence spectrum was then recorded at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm in the wavelength range of 350–650 nm. The fluorescence ratios (I600/I430) were calculated based on the fluorescence intensities at 600 and 430 nm. The excitation and emission slits were set at 10 nm and 5 nm, respectively. And the scanning speed was set to 120 nm/min. All measurements were repeated three times and then averaged.

A series of glucose solutions of 1.8 mM, 3.7 mM, 7.5 mM, 15 mM, 30 mM, 60 mM was prepared. To demonstrate the potential of the CDs/CdTe QDs in proton sensing produced by the enzymatic reaction, the concentrations of GOx was fixed at 1 mg/mL. After the substrate was added, the mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 1 h to complete the enzyme catalyzed reaction. After that, CDs and CdTe QDs were added as a fluorescent probe to monitor the production of protons in enzymatic reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CDs and CdTe QDs are obtained by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. 1a shows that the synthesized CDs are near spherical in shape and well distributed. Moreover, the spacing of their transparent lattice fringes is observed to be 0.25 nm indicating its good crystallinity (insets of Fig. 1a). The histogram in Fig. 1b exhibits that the size distribution of the as-prepared CDs is 2.36 to 3.65 nm, including uniform as well suitable particle size. Further, about 80% of the CDs are between 2.6 and 3.4, confirming the highly size selective synthesis process. As shown in Fig. 1c and d, the QDs are uniformly dispersed with an average particle size of 2.92 nm demonstrating the successful synthesis of QDs. HRTEM images show clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, suggesting the perfect crystalline nature of QDs. In order to further explore the optimal synthesis conditions and extremely strong fluorescent characteristics of CDs, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs under the optimal conditions is calculated to be 18.9% with quinine sulfate as the reference (see Table S2).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) were used to analyze the surface functional groups of CDs and valence states of the elements in CDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, the three peaks in the full range XPS spectrum confirm that the main components of CDs are the corresponding elements C, N and O. The results of XPS spectrum and FTIR spectrum are in good agreement (Fig. S1). Table S1 shows the atomic percentages of these elements. The fitted peaks of the XPS spectrum of O 1s, N 1s and C 1s are shown in Fig. 2b, c and d respectively. The high resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) could be fitted as two peaks, at 531.5 eV (CO) [17,25] and 532.5 eV (CO) [17,32] groups. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows two peaks at 399.69 and 401.25 eV, which can be attributed to the presence of pyrrolic N and Graphitic [17], respectively. As shown in Fig. 2d, the C1 band of CDs can be deconvoluted into three Gaussian peaks situated at 284.75, 286.09 and 287.74 eV, which confirms CC/CC, CO/CN and OC may exist separately [15,33]. Based on XPS and FTIR data, detailed information on surface chemistry can be obtained, and it is determined that there are many hydrophilic groups on the CDs surface therefore the CDs have remarkable solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescence system
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the chromophores on the surface of CDs, such as CC and CO, CDs has UV absorption. In Fig. 3a, the UV–Vis spectrum is identified by a black curve, which has a large absorption peak at the wavelength of 200–300 nm and ascribed to the π–π* transition as well the n–π* transition [34,35]. In addition, for the fluorescence spectrum, the blue curve is the emission spectrum. At an excitation wavelength of 330 nm, a strong fluorescence emission peak appears at the wavelength of 430 nm. As other luminescent carbon dots materials, the CDs demonstrates excitation-related FL behavior [32]. It is evident from Fig. 3b, as the excitation wavelength is increased from 270 nm to 430 nm, the intensity of the fluorescence emission peak initially increases and then decreases, and the emission peak red-shifted gradually, where the emission peak at 430 nm due to CDs has highest intensity at 350 nm excitation. From Fig. 3c which can illustrate CdTe QDs has a narrow fluorescence peak at the wavelength of 600 nm. Considering that both CdTe QDs and CDs have strong fluorescence emission peaks at the same excitation wavelength, we chose an excitation wavelength is 330 nm (Fig. S6). Under 330 nm UV excitation, CdTe QDs has showed strong red fluorescence. Based on thorough analysis of the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3f, it shows that as the pH is changed from 3 to 11, CdTe QDs's fluorescence intensity gradually increases at the wavelength of 600 nm whereas the CDs's fluorescence intensity does not change significantly (Fig. 3e).

CDs and CdTe QDs have different response characteristics to pH value. The former is insensitive and the latter is sensitive to pH. Mixed CDs and CdTe QDs produces dual fluorescence emission peaks, providing a unique platform for ratiometric pH sensing. After the CDs and CdTe QDs are mixed in a certain ratio, two fluorescence emission peaks appear at the wavelength of 430 nm and 600 nm under a single wavelength of ultraviolet excitation (Fig. 3d).

In addition, we assume that the synthesized GSH-coated CdTe QDs have uniformly distributed carboxylic acid groups outside, so the pH value of the solution affects the protonation state of the carboxyl group, thereby affecting the stability of the QDs in solution [36]. Under alkaline conditions, the carboxylic acid group carried by the CdTe-terminated molecule GSH is deprotonated and forms a negatively charged shell on the QDs surface. This effect may keep the stability of the solution by electrostatic repulsion between the quantum dots. Therefore, its fluorescence intensity is high (see Table S3). In acidic pH, the carboxylic acid group is protonated, the electrostatic repulsion between the quantum dots will be reduced, QD will tend to precipitate, form aggregates, and the fluorescence intensity will decrease accordingly [37,38]. Changing the pH of the solution can indeed change the photoluminescence intensity. And TEM images support the above conclusions (see Fig. S3).

In order to evaluate the pH sensor still functions under complex component conditions, a variety of biologically relevant chemicals (such as dsDNA, amino acids, BSA, and amides) are introduced into the system to explore interference effects. It can be seen from Fig. 4a, the biomolecules involved in the test include calf thymus dsDNA, D-histidine, BSA, d-glutamine, glycine, D-asparagine, l-glutamine, cysteine and D-glutamic acid (Calf thymus DNA 1 μg/mL, BSA, amides and amino acid 10 μM). Fig. 4a depicts the effect of various biomolecules on this method. When potential interfering substances are added, compared with the control, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity I430/I600 do not change significantly. However, when the system is involved in H+, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity decrease obviously. Therefore, even if other biological interference substances mentioned above exist, the method still has good selectivity.

The left panel of Fig. 4b depicts the effect of solution pH value on fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 430 nm (I430) and 600 nm (I600). As the pH value decreases, I600 gradually decreases, but I430 does not change. This result shows that the fluorescence of the synthesized CDs has an incredibly fast response to H+. Taking I430 as a reference, there is an excellent linear relationship between the I600/I430 ratio and the pH value, with a pH value ranging from 3 to 11 (Fig. 4b). The linear regression equation is Y = 0.1708X − 0.4275 and the linear dependence coefficient is 0.998.

In addition, we further applied the ratiometric fluorescence pH probe for monitoring the enzymatic reaction of proton production. An example as a typical and significant proton-producing enzyme reaction is taken: glucose oxidase (GOx) catalyzes glucose and oxygen to produce the gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide. It is inferred that due to the generation of protons during the reaction, the pH value decreases, which in turn leads to a decrease in the ratio fluorescence value. As shown in Fig. 5a, in the case of glucose oxidase immobilization, the glucose concentration increases from 1.8 mM to 120 mM. Since the number of protons generated during the reaction is directly proportional to the glucose concentration, the fluorescence peak at a wavelength of 600 nm gradually decreases. Due to the simultaneous existence of various substances such as glucose, glucose oxidase, gluconic acid, etc., the fluorescence peak value at the wavelength of 430 nm also changes. However, it can be seen from Fig. 5b that as the glucose concentration increases, the ratio of I600/I430 diminishes correspondingly and the blue fitted curve gradually flattens, because the protons are produced and the pH value is reduced. Fig. 5b also explains that after adding different concentrations of substrate when the enzyme never existed, the changes in I600/I430 are not significant and can be ignored (Red dots in the Fig. 5b). The fluorescence response dynamics of the ratiometric probe is shown in Fig. S9.

In summary, we have adopted a green and environmentally friendly solvothermal method to synthesize CDs with high quantum yield using chitosan. Construction of pH response ratiometric fluorescent probes by simple mixing of the CDs and CdTe QDs without the need for cross-linking agent. More usefully, the probe has a wide pH detection range of 3–11. Moreover, this ratiometric probe also has the requisite qualities for monitoring enzyme catalytic reactions by measuring pH variation during the reaction.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Yinyin Chen:Data curation, Writing - original draft.Chenxi Zhao:Data curation, Writing - original draft.Yanying Wang:Data curation, Writing - original draft.Hanbing Rao:Formal analysis.Zhiwei Lu:Formal analysis.Changfang Lu:Writing - review & editing.Zhi Shan:Writing - review & editing.Bi Ren:Writing - review & editing.Wei Wu:Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition.Xianxiang Wang:Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305097) and General Project of Education Department of Sichuan (Project No. 035Z2270).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2020.111264.
